title: Overlapping Approver Assignment: Deviation-Based vs Amount-Based Routing
description: Section 2.2(c) routes invoices to Finance Controller when invoice amount exceeds PO by >= 10%. However, Section 5.2 routes invoices between INR 1L–10L to Department Head. For an invoice in the 1L–10L range with >= 10% deviation, both rules fire with different approvers.
example_scenario: Invoice of INR 5,00,000 that exceeds PO by 12%: Section 2.2(c) says Finance Controller; Section 5.2 says Department Head.
recommendation: Define precedence: deviation-based routing (Section 2) should override amount-based routing (Section 5) when deviations are detected, since deviation handling is more specific. OR add explicit combined conditions.

title: CFO vs Finance Controller for High-Value Critical Deviations
description: Section 2.2(c) routes to Finance Controller for >= 10% amount deviation. Section 5.4 routes to CFO for invoices above INR 50L. For an invoice above INR 50L with >= 10% deviation, both rules assign different approvers.
example_scenario: Invoice of INR 60,00,000 with 15% deviation over PO: Section 2.2(c) says Finance Controller; Section 5.4 says CFO.
recommendation: Specify that for invoices in the CFO approval band, the CFO takes precedence. Finance Controller should be notified (CC) but not the primary approver.

title: Overlapping Routing: Deviation-Based (1–10%) vs Amount-Based (10L–50L)
description: Section 2.2(b) routes invoices with 1–10% deviation to Department Head. Section 5.3 routes invoices between INR 10L–50L to Finance Controller. An invoice of INR 15L with 5% deviation receives conflicting routing instructions.
example_scenario: Invoice of INR 15,00,000 with 5% over PO: Section 2.2(b) says Department Head; Section 5.3 says Finance Controller.
recommendation: Amount-based approval should take precedence over deviation-based routing when the deviation is minor (1–10%). Deviation-based escalation should only override amount-based routing for severe deviations (>= 10%).

title: Watchlist Vendor Exception vs Auto-Approve Rule
description: Section 5.1 auto-approves invoices up to INR 1L if all validations pass. Section 5.5 states watchlist vendors always require Department Head approval. While Section 5.5 explicitly calls itself an 'Exception to Section 5.1', the priority ordering is not machine-enforceable without explicit precedence rules.
example_scenario: Invoice of INR 50,000 from a watchlist vendor: Section 5.1 allows auto-approve; Section 5.5 requires Department Head.
recommendation: Rule AP-APR-005 (watchlist exception) should have higher priority than AP-APR-001 (auto-approve). The current baseline assigns AP-APR-005 priority=78 vs AP-APR-001 priority=80, which correctly gives the watchlist rule precedence.

title: Overlapping Amount Range: AP-VAL-003 vs AP-APR-002
description: Rules AP-VAL-003 (→ AP_MANAGER) and AP-APR-002 (→ DEPARTMENT_HEAD) both apply in the INR 100,000–1,000,000 range but assign different approvers.
example_scenario: Invoice of INR 550,000 triggers both rules.
recommendation: Define explicit precedence: deviation-based routing should supersede amount-based routing when a deviation is detected.

title: Overlapping Amount Range: AP-VAL-003 vs AP-APR-003
description: Rules AP-VAL-003 (→ AP_MANAGER) and AP-APR-003 (→ FINANCE_CONTROLLER) both apply in the INR 1,000,000–5,000,000 range but assign different approvers.
example_scenario: Invoice of INR 3,000,000 triggers both rules.
recommendation: Define explicit precedence: deviation-based routing should supersede amount-based routing when a deviation is detected.
